question: If I got a D in my prereq course, do I retake it or can I move on?
answer: You must retake it. A D does not meet the requirement of a C or better for a course used to satisfy a CS prerequisite.

question: I got an override error when trying to re-enroll in a course—where should I go?
answer: Registration override request details for repeat attempts can be found [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: Is a fourth try at a course even allowed for me as a veteran student?
answer: Repeat attempts are handled through a registration override request. Find the details [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: I failed a class twice, where can I find info on retaking it?
answer: Check the registration override request details for repeat attempts [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: How do I get an override to repeat a course?
answer: Registration override request details for repeat attempts are available [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: Between the candidacy milestone, the typical graduation time, and the hard cap, what are all the timeframes I should know before I commit?
answer: You must reach PhD candidacy within 6 years, full-time students usually graduate in 4-5 years, and the overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years.

question: I'm juggling a full-time job and the PhD—am I still allowed in the program if it's taken me 8 years so far?
answer: Yes, you can continue as long as you don't exceed the 9-year overall limit to graduate. Note that you also must have been advanced to PhD candidacy within 6 years.

question: Urgent: I'm at year 5 and behind—am I okay eligibility-wise to keep going?
answer: Yes. At year 5 you're within both limits—you have until year 6 to be advanced to candidacy and until year 9 overall to graduate.

question: I'm moving here from another state for the PhD. Step by step, how should I think about my time limits and what's a realistic graduation window?
answer: Step 1: Plan to advance to PhD candidacy within 6 years. Step 2: Keep in mind the overall cap of 9 years to graduate. Step 3: Realistically, full-time PhD students typically finish in 4-5 years, so aim for that range.

question: Need to submit a fourth attempt override fast. Where do I go?
answer: The details for registration override requests on repeat attempts are available [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: Quick question: where are the instructions for a repeat-attempt override before I miss the window?
answer: The instructions for registration override requests on repeat attempts can be found [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: If the registrar grants my reclassification, which is better—getting money back now or rolling the credit into spring? What exactly gets refunded?
answer: Both options are available when your appeal is granted. You can either request a refund for the difference between the in-state and out-of-state rates, or apply that credit balance to the next semester. What's refunded is that rate difference.

question: I just found out I got a D in my CS prerequisite and I register tomorrow morning - do I have to redo it right now?
answer: Yes. You must retake any course intended to satisfy a prerequisite for a CS course if you did not earn a C or better in it.

question: Do I have to take a refund, or can I just keep the credit for next term?
answer: You have both options. If your appeal is granted, you may request a refund for the in-state vs. out-of-state difference, or apply the credit balance to the next semester.

question: Will I be reimbursed for the tuition difference if my appeal succeeds?
answer: Yes. A granted appeal lets you request a refund for the difference between the in-state and out-of-state rates, or apply that credit toward the next semester.

question: I'm a veteran starting classes Monday and need to know who handles veteran certification fast - who do I contact?
answer: Reach out to the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for information on the veteran certification process and other veteran benefits.

question: As an active duty service member with a tight deadline, are there special domicile considerations I should check right now?
answer: Yes, there are. Review the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for details on military-specific domicile information.

question: I'm coming back to school and need urgent answers about military family benefits - where do I look?
answer: See the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page, and contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for questions about veteran benefits.

question: Time is short - does GMU make exceptions for active duty, veterans, or their families?
answer: Yes. Check the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for more information.

question: I need veteran benefit info quickly before I enroll - which office should I call?
answer: Contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for information regarding veteran benefits and the veteran certification process.

question: Hypothetically, if I were active duty military while in my MS program, would there be any special considerations I could weigh against the standard rules?
answer: Yes, exceptions exist for active duty, veterans, and military. See the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for details, and contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for veteran benefits or the certification process.

question: Say I had to choose between figuring out domicile on my own versus using a military pathway — what if I'm a veteran, are there options for me?
answer: Yes, there are military-specific options. Compare the details on the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page, and reach out to the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for veteran benefits or the certification process.

question: If I were trying to decide whether my military status changes anything for me, where's the best place to read up on it?
answer: Start with the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for the relevant information, then contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for veteran benefits or the certification process.

question: Do I go to the IST office in person to make an advising appointment, or is there an online portal?
answer: There's an online tool. Students schedule academic advising appointments using [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/).

question: I think I can take like 9 credits of CS 896 in one semester to speed things up—is that allowed?
answer: No, you can take at most 6 credits of CS 896 per semester. In total it can be repeated up to 18 credits.

question: What's the total cap on CS 896 credits I'm allowed to rack up over time?
answer: You can repeat CS 896 for up to 18 credits total, with no more than 6 credits taken in any single semester.

question: I'm coming from out of state and thought I could just sign up for 19 credits this fall — but the system blocked me. What's actually going on?
answer: IST advisors don't recommend more than 16 credits in a fall/spring semester, so anything above the max requires a credit overload request. For 19 credits, you must have at least a 3.0 cumulative GPA. You can submit the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request) if you meet that criteria.

question: I think I have like a 2.8 GPA and want to take 21 credits next term since I moved here for school. Will that be approved?
answer: Unfortunately, no. For requests of 20-22 credits, a student must have at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA. With a 2.8 you wouldn't meet the threshold (and 19 credits requires at least a 3.0). Previous academic performance and credit load are also factored into the decision.

question: I missed the add deadline because I was relocating from another state. Does that mean I can't request extra credits at all anymore?
answer: You can still try, but there's an extra step. The deadline for submission is the last day to add classes (see the GMU [Academic Calendar](https://registrar.gmu.edu/calendars/)). If you submit after that date, your request must be accompanied by a [Late Schedule Adjustment](https://registrar.gmu.edu/wp-content/uploads/LSA-Late-Schedule-Adjusment-9.2022.pdf).

question: My appeal was finally granted and I'm enrolled again soon—can the overpayment just roll forward instead of waiting on a refund?
answer: Yes. Since your appeal was granted, you can apply the credit balance to the next semester instead of requesting a refund of the difference between the in-state and out-of-state rates.

question: How do I actually file an overload request as a CEC student?
answer: Submit the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request). You must meet GPA criteria: at least a 3.0 cumulative GPA for 19 credits, and at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA for 20-22 credits. Your previous academic performance and credit load are also factored into the decision.

question: What GPA do I need to take 20 credits?
answer: For requests of 20-22 credits, you need at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA. For 19 credits, you need at least a 3.0 cumulative GPA.

question: When is the deadline to submit my overload request?
answer: The deadline is the last day to add classes (check the GMU [Academic Calendar](https://registrar.gmu.edu/calendars/)). If you submit after that date, your request must be accompanied by a [Late Schedule Adjustment](https://registrar.gmu.edu/wp-content/uploads/LSA-Late-Schedule-Adjusment-9.2022.pdf).

question: Can I request an overload to retake a class I failed?
answer: No. Requests for an overload to repeat courses will not be approved. Also, requests for an overload from students restricted to 13 credits will not be approved.

question: I'm weighing whether to load up on credits this fall or stay lighter — what's the recommended cap, and at what point do I actually need to file an overload request?
answer: IST academic advisors recommend no more than 16 credits in a fall/spring semester. If you want to go above the maximum allowed per semester, you'd need to submit a [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request). For 19 credits you need at least a 3.0 cumulative GPA; for 20-22 credits you need at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA.

question: Trying to decide between a heavy summer or a heavy fall — what are the credit limits for each, and how do they compare?
answer: For summers, advisors recommend no more than 7 credits at the same time. For fall/spring, the recommendation is no more than 16 credits. Going beyond the maximum allowed requires a [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request).

question: I'm comparing whether to retake a course as part of an overload or just add new ones — is repeating courses an option for an overload, and what about students capped at 13 credits?
answer: Overload requests to repeat courses will not be approved, so repeating a course is not a viable overload option. Additionally, requests for an overload from students restricted to 13 credits will not be approved. You can review the full process via the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request).

question: I need numbers right away to map my timeline: what's the absolute cap on finishing the CS PhD?
answer: The overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years. Within that, you must reach candidacy by year 6, though full-time students usually graduate in 4-5 years.

question: I'm deciding whether to commit—realistically, how soon could I expect to graduate if I go full-time?
answer: Full-time PhD students are usually able to graduate within 4-5 years, though the program allows up to 9 years total.

question: I'm an F-1 student and I heard there's some final date to send the credit overload form. When exactly do I need to submit it?
answer: The deadline to submit the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request) is the last day to add classes. Check the GMU [Academic Calendar](https://registrar.gmu.edu/calendars/) for that date.

question: For summer I want to take more than 7 credits at once. Is the timing deadline the same as fall?
answer: IST advisors don't recommend more than 7 credits at the same time in summers. For any overload request, the deadline is the last day to add classes per the GMU [Academic Calendar](https://registrar.gmu.edu/calendars/). Submit the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request).

question: Is it true you only get 6 years to fully complete the whole PhD?
answer: No. The 6-year limit is for being advanced to PhD candidacy, not completing the whole degree. The overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years.

question: I'm an in-state IST student living with my parents — am I even allowed to book an advising appointment, or is that just for transfer students?
answer: Yes, you can book one. Students may schedule academic advising appointments using [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/).

question: My mom told me advising appointments are only for seniors. Is that true, or can I schedule one too?

question: I'm getting nowhere trying to email a general advising address — is there even a central contact, or do I have to go through my specific program, and what about my friend who's in grad school?

question: I'm an out-of-state PhD student and I heard I can take like 24 credits of CS 896 toward my degree. Is that right?

question: How many credits can I take in fall before needing an overload?
answer: IST advisors don't recommend more than 16 credits in a fall/spring semester. To go above the maximum, submit a [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request) by the last day to add classes.

question: Where do I find the exact last day to add classes for overload timing?

question: Can I still request a summer overload, and what's the limit?

question: I urgently need to talk to someone about my program timeline — does it matter whether I'm a grad or undergrad student?

question: My parents want to know what office handles credit overloads and whether my 3.05 GPA is enough to take 21 credits. Can you clarify both?
answer: Credit overloads are handled through the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request) for CEC students. For 20-22 credits, you need at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA, so a 3.05 would not meet the requirement.

question: Comparing options, is it better to spread CS 896 across several semesters or try to do it all at once?

question: As an active-duty service member, what if my deployment slows me down — am I still allowed to finish the PhD if it takes me 8 years total?

question: As an active-duty student, what if it takes me 7 years just to reach candidacy — am I still eligible to continue?

question: Say I want to load up on 21 credits one term — would a 3.0 GPA be enough to qualify?

question: I'm a future applicant and not sure of the right steps to get advising. What should I do first?
answer: To set up academic advising, use [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/) to schedule your appointment.

question: Say I miss the normal window to ask for extra credits — would I still have any option?

question: As a parent, I'm wondering—what if my child takes longer than expected in the CS PhD program? Is there an absolute time limit?
answer: Yes. The overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years. Additionally, the student must be advanced to PhD candidacy within 6 years.

question: What if my daughter can't finish her PhD in the usual time? When does she absolutely have to be done by?
answer: The maximum overall time to graduate is 9 years. Most full-time PhD students, however, are able to graduate within 4-5 years.

question: What if things go smoothly for my child—how quickly do most full-time PhD students typically finish?
answer: Full-time PhD students are usually able to graduate within 4-5 years, with a maximum overall limit of 9 years and candidacy required within 6 years.

question: Is the 9-year limit for reaching candidacy or for finishing the whole degree?
answer: The 9-year limit is for finishing the whole degree - overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years. Reaching candidacy has a separate, earlier deadline: you must advance to candidacy within 6 years.

question: Being out-of-state, I'm trying to plan a heavy summer load before the deadline. How many credits can I take in summer and when is the request due?

question: As an international PhD student, if I max out CS 896 at the full credit limit, does that cover my dissertation requirements too?
answer: No. Even with the maximum 18 CS 896 credits, you must still complete the required credits for CS 998/999, along with CS 600, 700, 701, and 3 advanced graduate courses.

question: Can I take longer than 9 years to graduate?
answer: No, overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years. You must also be advanced to candidacy within 6 years.

question: I'm a vet and I think I'm an undergrad in science—does that mean I qualify to look up my own advisor myself?

question: I'm an online MS student and got a C- in a course I planned to use as a CS prerequisite. Can I just move on to the next CS course, or do I have to redo it?

question: Is there a deadline to complete the whole PhD program?

question: What does the 6-year rule mean in the PhD program?

question: Hypothetically, if my service obligations pulled me away for a while, is there a hard cap on the total years allowed in the program?

question: Say I had to balance reserve duty with coursework—how do the candidacy and graduation time limits work for me?

question: Since I'm transferring in, I want to confirm two things: is there a prerequisite for CS 896, and how many semesters worth of credits can I rack up?

question: My son is in the College of Engineering and Computing studying IST. What's the normal credit limit each semester, and is summer different?
answer: IST academic advisors do not recommend more than 16 credits in a fall/spring semester. In summers, they don't recommend more than 7 credits at the same time. To go above the maximum, your son would need to submit a [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request).

question: Could you walk me through both the GPA thresholds and the deadline so I can help my daughter request more credits?
answer: For GPA: 19 credits requires at least a 3.0 cumulative GPA, and 20–22 credits requires at least a 3.10 cumulative GPA. The deadline to submit the [Credit Overload request form](https://gmu683-workflow2.elluciancloud.com/webentry/29/student_overload_request) is the last day to add classes (see the GMU [Academic Calendar](https://registrar.gmu.edu/calendars/)). If submitted later, a [Late Schedule Adjustment](https://registrar.gmu.edu/wp-content/uploads/LSA-Late-Schedule-Adjusment-9.2022.pdf) must be included.

question: I'm paying out-of-state tuition, but my dad is active duty military. Are there different domicile options for military families compared to regular students?
answer: Yes, there are special provisions. Check the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for details. For questions about veteran benefits or the certification process, contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu).

question: I think I read somewhere that veterans get the same rate as in-state students automatically — is that how it works versus the normal domicile rules?
answer: There are military-specific domicile provisions, but you'll want to review the specifics on the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page rather than assume. For veteran benefits or certification questions, contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu).

question: I'm trying to figure out whether to apply as a regular out-of-state student or claim some kind of military benefit — where do I compare those?
answer: For the military-related options, see the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page. The [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) can help with veteran benefits and the certification process.

question: Suppose I need advising guidance soon as a provisional MS student—where do I go to book a time?
answer: Use [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/) to schedule your academic advising appointment.

question: As a new transfer, I want to meet with someone before registration closes—do undergrads and grad students go to the same person, and where do I start?
answer: It depends on your level. Undergraduate students should find their advisor through the [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising), while graduate students should contact the Graduate Programs Director in the Office of Academic Affairs.

question: I'm transferring in and not sure if I need an advisor meeting early—who advises College of Science students, and does it differ if I'm a grad student?
answer: The college advises students through its departments and programs. Undergraduates use the advisor [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising); graduate students should reach out to the Graduate Programs Director in the Office of Academic Affairs.

question: I tried signing up for 8 credits of CS 896 this semester but the system blocked me - what's the limit again?
answer: The system is correct to block you - you can only take at most 6 credits of CS 896 per semester. Over time you can repeat it up to 18 credits total.

question: My registration for CS 896 got rejected and I'm not sure why. I don't think I've taken any breadth courses yet - could that be it?
answer: Yes, that's likely the issue. You must complete the breadth requirement first before you're allowed to enroll in CS 896.

question: With limited time before active duty, should I load up on CS 896 first or knock out other requirements? What's required either way?
answer: You must complete the breadth requirement before you can even enroll in CS 896. And no matter how many CS 896 credits you earn, you still need CS 600, 700, 701, three advanced graduate courses, plus the required CS 998/999 credits—so CS 896 won't replace those.

question: As a veteran, do I have to use a separate military advising system to book an advising session, or is there one tool everyone uses?
answer: You can use [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/) to schedule your academic advising appointment.

question: I'm under the gun to sort out my residency status as a transferring veteran—what's the fastest official resource for this?

question: I'm applying now and panicking — is there a hard cutoff for how long the CS PhD can take before they kick you out?
answer: Yes. You must be advanced to PhD candidacy within 6 years, and your overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years. That said, full-time PhD students usually finish in 4-5 years.

question: I just saw a '9 year' figure and freaked out — does that mean the program forces everyone to take 9 years?
answer: No, that's just the maximum. Overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years, but full-time PhD students are usually able to graduate within 4-5 years. You also must advance to candidacy within 6 years.

question: As an out-of-state student weighing my options, could I just enroll in CS 896 right away instead of other coursework?
answer: Not right away—you need to first complete the breadth requirement before enrolling in CS 896. After that, you can take it up to 6 credits per semester.

question: When I get to campus, who handles my advising?
answer: The college advises students through its various departments and programs. As an undergrad, you can find your advisor on this [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising).

question: I'm new here as a transfer—hypothetically, what would I need to do if a repeat attempt required an override?
answer: You'd follow the registration override request process for repeat attempts, with full details available [here](https://gmuedu.sharepoint.com/sites/CECISTDepartment-GRP/SitePages/Academics.aspx#registration-overrides).

question: What if I'm pursuing my doctorate and want to confirm—am I allowed to schedule advising through Patriot Connect?
answer: Yes, students can schedule academic advising appointments using [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/).

question: My situation is urgent and I don't even know who to talk to in the College of Science - where do I start?
answer: It depends on your level: undergraduate students find their advisor on the [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising), while graduate students contact the Graduate Programs Director in the Office of Academic Affairs.

question: Two issues as a new out-of-state College of Science student: first, the advising page won't load for me, and second, I don't know whether I follow undergrad or grad advising steps. How do I sort this out?
answer: Advising is handled through the college's departments and programs. If you're an undergraduate, find your advisor via the [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising). If you're a graduate student, contact the Graduate Programs Director in the Office of Academic Affairs.

question: What would the veteran certification process look like for me as a remote student?
answer: For information about the veteran certification process, contact the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu). You can also see the [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page for more details.

question: Imagine I want to learn about other veteran benefits while doing my online degree—where would I even start?
answer: Start with the [Office of Military Services](http://military.gmu.edu) for information about other veteran benefits and the veteran certification process. The [Active Duty, Veterans and Military](https://registrar.gmu.edu/students/domicile/military-specific-domicile-information/) page also has more information.

question: Hypothetically, if I'm not sure whether I count as an undergrad or grad student, how does the College of Science route me to the right person?
answer: It depends on your level: undergraduates locate their advisor via the advising [list](https://science.gmu.edu/academics/advising-student-support/advising), while graduate students reach out to the Graduate Programs Director in the Office of Academic Affairs.

question: If I load up on CS 896, do I still owe the core courses?
answer: Yes. No matter how many CS 896 credits you earn, you still need CS 600, 700, 701, three advanced graduate courses, and the required CS 998/999 credits.

question: Something flagged my record about exceeding program time — what are the actual maximum years allowed?
answer: There are two limits: you must reach PhD candidacy within 6 years, and your overall time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years.

question: I'm comparing a fast track versus a longer path through the PhD — what's the typical full-time completion time and what's the hard maximum?
answer: Full-time PhD students usually graduate within 4-5 years, but the overall maximum time to graduate cannot exceed 9 years.

question: Suppose I'm coming back to school while working full-time and need to talk with an advisor—what's the actual tool I'd use to set that up?
answer: You'd use [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/) to schedule your academic advising appointment.

question: What if I wanted to understand the basic process for booking an advising session as a returning student—where does it start?
answer: The process starts with [Patriot Connect](https://connect.gmu.edu/), which is where you schedule academic advising appointments.
